current_fact.fact_text: Some historians of music like to mark May 7 as a kind of invisible festival: Beethoven’s Ninth in 1824, Popov’s 'radio day' in 1895, and Sony’s founding in 1946 all fall on this date. Taken together, they trace a line from orchestras in concert halls to signals in the air to songs in your pocket.

episode.title: Morning Drops #12 - 80's Club
episode.description: Starting with a Monday evening in Munich — a few drinks, friends, and landing in an 80s dance party. From Donna Summer's hypnotic pulse, to Depeche Mode's bright synthpop, Ken Laszlo's Italo Disco romance, and Mylène Farmer's darker theatrical world. Four songs, four corners of the 80s — connected by the machines that changed how we dance.
songs: Depeche Mode - Just Can't Get Enough

current_fact.match_reason: An invisible festival of orchestras, radio, and electronics culminates beautifully in an 80s club set where machines literally reshape how humans gather and dance.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1992, Space Shuttle Endeavour launched on its maiden voyage, STS-49. The mission famously featured the first and only three‑astronaut spacewalk to capture and repair the stranded Intelsat VI satellite, showcasing the improvisational side of human spaceflight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A shuttle’s first flight and a triple spacewalk pair naturally with an episode about seeing emptiness as creative space, echoing both the literal space environment and the feeling of new beginnings.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1895, Russian physicist Alexander Popov demonstrated a lightning detector in Saint Petersburg, effectively one of the earliest radio receivers. In parts of the former Soviet Union, this date is still celebrated as Radio Day, honoring the birth of wireless communication.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A primitive radio receiver born from lightning fits perfectly with an 'Electric Shock' set, tying together electricity, transmission, and the thrill of early tech.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1824, Beethoven’s Ninth Symphony premiered in Vienna. By then almost completely deaf, Beethoven reportedly followed along with the orchestra from the stage and had to be gently turned toward the audience to see the thunderous applause he could no longer hear.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: The grandeur and emotional arc of Beethoven’s Ninth resonates with the cinematic, neo-classical mood of an episode about whales and vast, moving soundscapes.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1930, a magnitude 7.1 earthquake struck near Salmas in northwestern Iran, reaching a Mercalli intensity of IX and destroying many villages. The quake not only reshaped the landscape but also catalyzed early seismic studies in the region, helping scientists better understand how the Earth moves.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A violent reshaping of the earth connects with an episode about weathering storms and finding constancy through upheaval, both geological and emotional.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1942, the Battle of the Coral Sea saw two enemy fleets clash without any of their ships seeing one another; aircraft launched from carriers did all the fighting. It was the first naval battle fought entirely by planes, foreshadowing a new era of remote, technology-driven warfare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: This quietly revolutionary battle, where tactics had to be re-learned around unseen enemies and aircraft carriers, pairs with an episode about adapting to new paradigms and the difficult joy of learning through trial and error.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1986, Canadian mountaineer Patrick Morrow became the first person to climb the Seven Summits, the highest peak on each continent. His feat demanded not just physical endurance but also years of planning, patience, and surviving wildly different climates and cultures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A mountaineer circling the globe's highest peaks aligns with an episode anchored in mountain landscapes, blending stories of altitude, perspective, and the quiet obsession of climbing.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1960, Soviet leader Nikita Khrushchev announced that the USSR had captured U.S. U‑2 pilot Gary Powers, shattering the illusion that the spy plane was invulnerable. The revelation embarrassed the United States and intensified Cold War tensions over surveillance and trust.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A story about high‑altitude surveillance and the anxiety of being watched mirrors an episode questioning our hyper-connected lives and the invisible networks that track us.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1945, the last German U-boat attack of World War II sank two freighters off Scotland’s Firth of Forth, even as the war in Europe was ending. It was a final, almost anachronistic echo of a conflict that had already been decided.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

published.match_reason: A final wartime attack off the Firth of Forth connects geographically and emotionally with an episode rooted in Scottish skies and transitions at the edge of eras.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1946, Tokyo Telecommunications Engineering was founded in a bombed‑out department store in Japan; it would later rename itself Sony. Starting with repairing radios and making tape recorders, the company grew into one of the most influential forces in electronics, music, and gaming.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: A tiny postwar electronics shop that grew into a music‑shaping giant pairs with an episode about another subtle technology—Auto‑Tune—that transformed how we hear and produce sound.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1940, the Norway Debate in the British House of Commons exposed deep dissatisfaction with Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain’s handling of the war. Within three days, he resigned and Winston Churchill took over, altering the leadership and rhetoric of Britain’s fight against Nazi Germany.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A parliamentary revolt that changes leadership aligns with an episode steeped in political resistance and critique, both narratives about speaking against the status quo.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1915, German submarine U‑20 torpedoed the ocean liner RMS Lusitania off the coast of Ireland, killing 1,199 people. The sinking shocked global public opinion and helped shift U.S. sentiment sharply against Germany long before America officially entered World War I.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A tragic story of a German submarine and an ocean liner is mirrored, in a gentler, healing way, by an episode about Germans at sea, focusing on shared humanity instead of conflict.

published.fact_text: Also on May 7, 1915, the government of the Republic of China agreed to most of Japan’s infamous Twenty-One Demands. The concessions extended Japanese control over Manchuria and large parts of the Chinese economy, fueling nationalist resentment that would shape Chinese politics for decades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: An episode about lyrical resistance and identity suits a moment when foreign demands reshaped a nation’s sovereignty and sparked long-term nationalist pushback.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1794, during the French Revolution, Maximilien Robespierre introduced the Cult of the Supreme Being as the new state religion of the French Republic. It was an attempt to replace traditional faith with a rational, civic spirituality—complete with festivals, symbolism, and its own theology.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A state‑crafted civic religion with festivals and rituals pairs with an episode about a deeply ritualized musical tradition, both exploring how culture turns belief and emotion into performance.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1765, the Royal Navy launched HMS Victory at Chatham Dockyard, though she would not be commissioned until 1778. Decades later she became famous as Admiral Nelson’s flagship at the Battle of Trafalgar, turning a wooden warship into a floating national symbol.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A legendary sailing warship fits naturally with a sea-bound episode, contrasting the grim business of battle with modern stories of shared voyages and calm oceans.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1999, a NATO airstrike mistakenly hit the Chinese embassy in Belgrade during the Kosovo War, killing three Chinese journalists and injuring many others. The incident sparked protests and a diplomatic crisis, revealing how fragile global trust can be when guided by imperfect information.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A tragic miscalculation in a foreign city is balanced by an episode about mindful travel and seeing more clearly, hinting at how perspective and attention can change our impact abroad.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1952, British engineer Geoffrey Dummer first publicly described the concept of the integrated circuit—a whole electronic circuit printed on a tiny piece of semiconductor. His idea laid the conceptual foundation for microchips, the hidden architecture inside nearly every modern device.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: The birth of the microchip, which made portable music players possible, pairs with an episode that explicitly reflects on returning to an iPod and rethinking how circuits and algorithms shape our listening.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1920, Polish forces led by Józef Piłsudski captured Kyiv in the Polish–Soviet War, only to be driven out weeks later by a Red Army counteroffensive. The brief occupation turned the city into a contested symbol between competing visions of Eastern Europe’s future.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A historical Polish offensive into Eastern Europe resonates with an episode about crossing modern Polish borders and landscapes, reframing contested ground through contemporary travel and music.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1685, the Battle of Vrtijeljka saw Venetian‑backed rebels face Ottoman forces in present‑day Montenegro. The clash was part of the wider Great Turkish War and revealed how local uprisings and imperial ambitions intertwined in the rugged Balkans.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

published.match_reason: A little-known Balkan battle meets an episode that literally features Balkan brass and urges us to re‑examine old conflicts, narratives, and inherited beliefs.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1940s-era Sony’s corporate ancestor wasn’t the only tech story on this date; over a century later, exploding conflicts still rewrite technology’s role. On May 7, 2025, Indian armed forces reportedly launched Operation SINDOOR, a series of surgical strikes on terrorist hideouts in Pakistan following the Pahalgam attack, blending precision air power with ground operations in a tightly choreographed response.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A modern military operation involving precision strikes aligns with an episode about martial training and discipline, connecting large‑scale conflict to the embodied art of combat sports.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 2004, American contractor Nick Berg was abducted and beheaded by Islamist militants in Iraq, with the act filmed and released online. The shocking video became one of the first widely shared examples of extremist propaganda in the age of broadband internet, changing how violence was mediated and witnessed globally.

episode.title: Morning Drops #55 - JOMO
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores one of her own personality traits through the lens of psychology and music: the Joy of Missing Out. 

From the Need for Cognition to Deliberate Ignorance and Inattentional Blindness, the episode traces what it means to be someone whose brain demands depth before it gives energy, and what happens when the world around you expects the opposite.

Listen at your own pace, and let us know if you recognize yourself in this one.

Tracklist:
Burna Boy - Ye
2Pac - I Ain't Mad at Cha
Jet Life - Paper Habits
Travis Scott - Stop Trying to Be God
Andrea Laszlo De Simone - Il Regno Animale

published.match_reason: The weaponization of online video contrasts with an episode advocating deliberate limits on what we consume, suggesting a counter‑practice to the constant exposure of the early 2000s internet.

published.fact_text: On May 7, 1945’s U-boat epilogue wasn’t the only maritime story tied to larger shifts; decades later, cultural currents would travel in different ways. From Beethoven’s chorus to flamenco palos, May 7 has become an unofficial day of listening experiments on Earth—tying back to Popov’s 1895 lightning detector and the birth of radio as a new way of sharing culture across oceans of air.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The emergence of radio as a cultural medium matches Sedna FM’s very first broadcast, a 'November Bomb' of sound that, like early radio, launched a new shared listening space.

published.fact_text: On May 7, the Earth’s history also remembers how technology, art, and geography keep weaving together: from integrated circuits to synthesisers, from Popov’s receiver to Afrobeat crossing continents. Each of these quiet inventions turned invisible patterns—electricity, rhythm, signal—into something humans could feel.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: The story of circuits and signals finding their way into music fits with an episode tracing Afrobeat’s evolution, where electronics and traditional rhythms merge into a global sound.

published.fact_text: By coincidence, May 7 gathers events that all orbit around control—of seas, skies, circuits, and stories. From the U‑2 sky to integrated chips and Auto‑Tuned vocals, Earth keeps revisiting the same question: how much do we shape our tools, and how much do they end up shaping us back?

A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: An hour that synthesizes Cold War skies, microchips, and mediated lives is a natural echo for an episode questioning our entanglement with the devices those eras birthed.
